turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion in case 16-1276, Digital Realty Trust v. Somers.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Aiming to curtain corporate fraud, Congress passed the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 and the 2010 Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act. Both Acts shield whistleblowers from retaliation, but they differ in important respects. Sarbanes-Oxley applies to all employees who report misconduct to any of multiple recipients, the Securities and Exchange Commission, any other federal agency, Congress, or an internal supervisor. Dodd-Frank defines whistleblower to mean one who provides information relating to a violation of the securities laws to the Commission. A person who fits Dodd-Frank's definition of whistleblower is eligible for an award if original information he or she provides to the SEC yields a successful enforcement action. Persons conveying information to the SEC and therefore qualifying as whistleblowers under Dodd-Frank are protected from retaliation for among other things making disclosures required or protected under Sarbanes-Oxley, the Securities Exchange Act or other federal securities laws. The question presented in this case, does the anti-retaliation provision of Dodd-Frank cover an individual who made no report to the SEC and therefore falls outside the Act's definition of whistleblower? Under regulation promulgated by the SEC, an individual need not tell the SEC to qualify for protection. We hold that the statute calls for the opposite answer. To sue under Dodd-Frank's anti-retaliation provision a person must as the Act says provide information relating to a violation of the securities laws to the Commission. In the case before us, plaintiff respondent, Paul Somers, sued his former employer Digital Realty in Federal District Court. Somers asserted that his employment had been terminated in retaliation for his whistleblowing and that Digital Realty had therefore violated Dodd-Frank. Digital Realty moved to dismiss, arguing that although Somers reported alleged fraud internally, he made no disclosure to the SEC. The District Court denied the motion to dismiss and the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit affirmed. We granted review to resolve a division of opinion among Courts of Appeals on what it takes to gain anti-retaliation protection under Dodd-Frank. Dodd-Frank's whistleblower provision is designed to aid the Commission's enforcement efforts by motivating people who know of securities laws violations to tell the SEC. To that end, Congress narrowly defined who is eligible for the statute's benefits, namely a whistleblower who provides pertinent information to the Commission. The anti-retaliation provision then describes what conduct when engaged in by a whistleblower is shielded from retaliation. Dodd-Frank thus protects individuals, like Somers, who report alleged fraud to an internal supervisor, but only if they also provide information to the Commission. Because Congress has spoken plainly and directly to the question at issue, we accord no deference to the contrary view advanced by the SEC in its rulemaking. In short, Somers did not inform the SEC, so Dodd-Frank does not protect him. Because the Court of Appeals ruled otherwise, we reverse its judgment. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment, in which Justices Alito and Gorsuch have joined. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Breyer has joined.
